title: Today's AI Brief: Adobe’s conversational AI agent is a mediocre design intern
summary: A look at Adobe's conversational AI agent and Illinois' new AI safety bill
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for Adobe’s conversational AI agent is a mediocre design intern
imageCaption: Adobe’s conversational AI agent is a mediocre design intern (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI Brief. Let's dive into the news. First up, Adobe has launched a conversational AI agent called Firefly. It's designed to help with design tasks, but from what I've seen, it's more like a mediocre design intern. It's fun to watch it work, but it's no professional. I mean, who hasn't had a design intern who's still figuring things out?

According to The Verge, Firefly is Adobe's attempt at creating a conversational AI that can help with design tasks. While it's not quite up to par with a human designer or photo editor, it's an interesting experiment. I see this as a platform shift in how we interact with design tools. The economics of AI models are such that they're becoming more accessible, but the data boundaries are still being figured out. It's like watching a kid learn to ride a bike – a bit wobbly, but getting there.

Next, Illinois lawmakers have passed a bill requiring frontier AI labs like OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google DeepMind to have their safety practices audited by a third party. This is a big deal, as it shows that governments are taking AI safety seriously. My read is that this will have a significant impact on how these labs operate and the trust we place in their models. It's reassuring to see lawmakers taking proactive steps to ensure AI is developed responsibly.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
